cons: Pay and annual raises/bonuses are not great since this position isn’t a truly client forward facing position. Workload can vary between extremely heavy to none at all. EFA directors/Operating group don’t fully listen to opinions or suggestions of those at lower level and pat themselves on the back for how “good” everything is. Hard to get promoted beyond Sr. Consultant, Manager and Sr. Manager level is based more on company need than actual employee skills.  Very niche side of FP&A so the work can be monotonous and not challenging after you get the understanding of it. Reporting tools like Beacon and SAP AND SWIFT can be a real pain to use. Client facing Partners and MD’s can sometimes show little respect for our analysis and the value EFA brings to the company. As with any big company raises and promotions are standardized across the board, so can be a struggle to see your actual impact on larger scale.

pros: Benefits package is comprehensive: Health benefits in particular offer better than average coverage. Excellent PTO allowance and rollover. More company holidays than most employers.
cons: The management team specific to my area were a tight-knit group who created the team I was on several years prior. Somewhere along the way (years before my arrival), processes by the dozens were breaking and not being mitigated properly. Incoming "individual contributors" (IC's) were getting blamed for issues that started years prior and management continued the cycle of promoting ineptitude within the lower ranks. IC like myself and others on my team would then receive negative performance reviews, which prevented us from transferring to other areas within Deloitte if desired. When I started, my grossly understaffed team had 4 members. 13 months later, I was the only one left. The team was back up to 6 three months later. Fast forward another 6 months, and the team was back down to 2. All but one of the seven who left the team also left Deloitte.

pros: Plenty of interesting projects to work on, a lot with decent hours. Pay is fine considering expectations and industry (Government)
cons:
My experience may be anomalous, but it was clear from early on that Deloitte did not know how to manage software projects or products.

When I first joined, I worked on a project where 3 of the teams ended up doing the exact same work. The director overseeing the project seemed unaware of this until teams were demoing their progress.

I've seen people who don't seem to have ever worked on a software oriented project leading operations and development. Staff on projects often don't seem concerned with the impact of their work, they take directions and implement them, even if the results don't add up to anything usable. They don't question instructions, they don't think critically about what is actually needed to produce a usable end-product that users will benefit from using. In short, too many people at Deloitte don't have any common sense - and they hire subcontractors who also don't have any common sense.

There are a handful of gems at Deloitte, but Deloitte has no idea how to value them.

title: Joined as a frontend developer on their "Project Delivery Model" track, meaning I had zero "firm-related obligations" and was expected just to deliver "client value" (via software development). This is largely my experience after 3 years, so the info was accurate. Most of the projects I've been on have been places to hone my development skills and do actual software development with competent teams and competent (or hands-off) management. All of my projects have been Federal or State-level contracts. My experience has been me being 100% focused on the projects themselves–Deloitte itself generally stays in the background and I can focus on the webapp or whatever I'm actually working on on the day-to-day, which feels great. The pay is *good enough*, and I get 5.54hr PTO accrual every 2-weeks. Holidays aren't generous imo. We lose President's Day, Veterans Day etc. in exchange for having Dec 25-Jan 1st off every year. Which is cool but really loads all of your holiday time off towards the Winter which is idk fine. If you're on a good project and/or are good at the technical work you do, you won't be working high-octane 8hr days (with some exceptions). It's chill, and satisfying.
pros:
Joined as a frontend developer on their "Project Delivery Model" track, meaning I had zero "firm-related obligations" and was expected just to deliver "client value" (via software development). This is largely my experience after 3 years, so the info was accurate. Most of the projects I've been on have been places to hone my development skills and do actual software development with competent teams and competent (or hands-off) management.
All of my projects have been Federal or State-level contracts.
My experience has been me being 100% focused on the projects themselves–Deloitte itself generally stays in the background and I can focus on the webapp or whatever I'm actually working on on the day-to-day, which feels great.
The pay is *good enough*, and I get 5.54hr PTO accrual every 2-weeks. Holidays aren't generous imo. We lose President's Day, Veterans Day etc. in exchange for having Dec 25-Jan 1st off every year. Which is cool but really loads all of your holiday time off towards the Winter which is idk fine.

If you're on a good project and/or are good at the technical work you do, you won't be working high-octane 8hr days (with some exceptions). It's chill, and satisfying.
cons:
Although technically a consultant, I only do software dev work, and am on an aggressive "client-deliverable" career track, so my experience is from that perspective.

The experience here lives and dies by the project you're on. And projects live and die by a combination of management quality, and "market conditions." In 3 years I've been on 5 projects. 2 ended due to mgmt incompetence and 2 ended due to market conditions.
Unsurprisingly you can work with incredible people. But as a software dev, you have much less control over management conditions. The company seems built around the non-technical consultants, so folks like me get paid a steady salary and are best left alone to just build things. But if you want to move up in the company, get meaningful raises etc. then you really can't be a software dev and have to pivot towards the kinds of roles that have people asking "what does....Deloitte actually do. What do you do all day...?" (meetings. it's meetings)

By far the worst part of Deloitte is more of a feature of "software devs in consulting" but nonetheless: lack of job stability. When you're hired, you get put on one project for free. If you choose to leave that project (or more likely, get cut) then it is *your* responsibility to market yourself and find a new role within the company otherwise you're fired.
This is talked about as a positive, or at least as something that fits in with consulting skills, but if you're a software dev you probably don't have a wide network of folks to help you out...because you're working on software, not networking with non-technical folks. So then you get to search through a constantly-changing (but always bad) internal marketplace app for a new role that hopefully was written/edited by a technical person to find a new role befitting your technical skills. You do an informal interview and transition to the new project, and then (hopefully) do more software dev until the next "suddenly risking unemployment" crisis.

TL;DR The software dev work itself is pretty good. Fulfilling, interesting, etc. The stability and structure around "getting to do the software dev you were hired for" is very bad.

title: Learn how to make PowerPoints well. Exposure to some interesting industries. Some pretty nice people.
pros: Learn how to make PowerPoints well. Exposure to some interesting industries. Some pretty nice people.
cons:
Where to start?
- laid off right before bonus
- you’ll succeed if you’re a type A workaholic that is willing to screw over other people to make yourself look good and get ahead, suck up to leadership, and need to be pat on the head like a dog and told “good boy” for getting all your assignments done.
- 90% of the firm doesn’t do any real work. They’re literally just making PowerPoint presentations everyday. Constantly having check in meetings talking in circles about “go to market” strategies and blah blah.
- any real work is done by cheap offshore developers or third party subs and vendors that get the worst rates so Deloitte can charge an obscene markup
- the stupidity of big companies paying this firm money to run projects is one of the greatest cons in the corporate world. $200+/hr for status updates and meeting minutes. 
- one vindictive person that writes a bad review will screw your career there
- tons of BS admin work to treat like a a grade schooler that’s scolded when you’re “not compliant”
- clueless PPMDs that are “rockstars” bc why? They don’t know their head from their….
- anyone that likes this place is a suck up that has no real identity or personality.
- all the “we are family one firm” talk is rubbish. The minute PPMDs see a market slowdown and losing money they’ll lay ppl off so they can get their bonuses to buy one more watch or sports car. 
- all the DEI and inclusion stuff is nonsense too. It’s all window dressing. No one cares about any of that.
- no one care that they do poor work for clients. They’ll happily bill unnecessarily and waste client money when they know they can do something faster bc “welp that’s what we signed”. 
- Zero integrity. Just make money no matter what.

cons: The general culture is EXTREMELY CLOSE MINDED especially while integrating senior level roles. I had multiple conversations with new partners and SMs and they told me that it took them years to feel fully integrated. I came in with 15+ years of Big 4 experience and they still couldn't trust me to lead projects, which in turn impacted my utilization, and ultimately they used that as an excuse for a layoff. Another important point. They hired me knowing full well that I was a new mom with a two year old. They told me they will help me to integrate considering that I might not be able to spend an 80 hour work week. But after getting hired I found that this was ABSOLUTELY NOT TRUE. I was pitted against male senior managers, none of whom were in my situation and could work way more hours than I could, and on way more opportunities and pursuits. So I found myself in a really bad situation and ultimately I just knew that this would end up with them letting me go. So I took a "do nothing, say nothing approach" and waited for fate to take its course.

title: In general, people and culture at Deloitte really are pretty good. Obviously there are exceptions and YMMV, but in general the company is filled with smart, competent, and personable people. You can learn a lot here - the resources and connections are overflowing. You get a lot of flexibility to try out different things, and you have a lot of agency in steering your career. Deloitte maybe doesn't have everything and maybe isn't the best at all it does, but it gets pretty close across the board. The people and systems and practices here are almost always professional and well organized. In terms of how companies are run, it doesn't get that much better than here.
pros: In general, people and culture at Deloitte really are pretty good. Obviously there are exceptions and YMMV, but in general the company is filled with smart, competent, and personable people. You can learn a lot here - the resources and connections are overflowing. You get a lot of flexibility to try out different things, and you have a lot of agency in steering your career. Deloitte maybe doesn't have everything and maybe isn't the best at all it does, but it gets pretty close across the board. The people and systems and practices here are almost always professional and well organized. In terms of how companies are run, it doesn't get that much better than here.
cons:
The size and scope of this company, while typically an advantage, can also be a bit overwhelming and hard to navigate. You can sometimes feel like a number, and the layers of corporate bureaucracy can feel really alienating and asinine. Messaging from leadership can feel cryptic and filtered that it feels disingenuous, and it doesn't always feel like management or leadership is looking out for you unless you build those relationships. It can be hard to get clarity about things like your performance ratings, compensation, etc. because you can't actually just get the answer from one person. 
It can also be harder than you think to steer your career the way you want to. Movement doesn't always feel encouraged, and inertia tends to be high unless you really push against it. Deloitte has an interest in keeping you in your siloes but they won't tell you that.

title: An array of client companies in a variety of industries Will get experience in auditing as well as insight into how companies operate
pros:
An array of client companies in a variety of industries

Will get experience in auditing as well as insight into how companies operate
cons:
Extreme working hours with no additional compensation.  I worked 3 straight months of 80+ hour weeks (one week I worked 90 hours), Saturdays and Sundays included, within my first 6 months with the firm.   

In addition to the client work hours, there are numerous hours of independent learning tasks that each professional must complete outside of their workday on their own time.  These are not so time-consuming when you are at the staff level, but as you progress to senior level, these become pretty significantly time-consuming.

You could be put on a client in a different state than where your home office is and have to travel via plane to the remote office for months on end. This is not widely publicized, so just be aware.

There is poor workforce deployment management and a dishonest representation of the seasonal nature of the workload.  They boast the idea that there is a 2-month busy season but that summers are extremely calm, and you will be able to take advantage of their vacation policies almost whenever you like during the long calm period.  What they don't tell you is that busy seasons are based on client filing timelines, which are happening at various times throughout the whole year based on the client;  there is always a busy season going on at any point during the year. The reality is that they can (and will) move you from one busy season client to another to another so that you are constantly working well over the normal 40 hours and so that taking a vacation would be "frowned upon" and would not receive approval since your client is in its busy season.  Please note that this happened to me when I knew of at least three other employees at my level who were on unassigned time and did not have a client. 

There is a mindset with many of the higher employees at this location that harms their ability to train/teach staff, resulting in staff that are poorly developed for their roles when it comes to rising to higher levels within the company (which occurs fairly quickly).  I (and others of my teammates at my level) even experienced a 3rd-year senior frequently giving blatantly incorrect instructions on tasks (either because they didn't know what to do and wouldn't admit it, or because they wanted to make the staff look bad). 

And finally, please don't let them convince you that this is a truly consistently  "apprenticeship style" job.  For some, it is, but for others, it is not.
